votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the square of one side equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
The Python code correctly implements the right angle triangle specification by checking if the sum of squares of any two sides equals the square of the third side.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the square of one side is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq spec. Both check if res equals the boolean result of testing whether any of the three Pythagorean combinations (a²+b²=c², a²+c²=b², b²+c²=a²) holds. The Coq uses orb and Z.eqb which correspond directly to Python's 'or' and '==' operators on integers. The order of evaluation and short-circuit behavior don't affect the final boolean result.

The Python code correctly computes the same boolean expression as the Coq spec. Both check if any of the three Pythagorean combinations hold (a²+b²=c², a²+c²=b², or b²+c²=a²) and verify that res equals this expected value. The orb operations in Coq correspond to Python's 'or', and Z.eqb corresponds to Python's '=='. Python's arbitrary precision integers match Coq's Z type for concrete computations.

The Python code correctly computes the same boolean expression as the Coq spec. Both check if any of the three Pythagorean combinations hold (a²+b²=c², a²+c²=b², or b²+c²=a²) and verify that res equals this expected value. The orb operations in Coq correspond to Python's 'or', and Z.eqb corresponds to Python's '=='. Python's arbitrary precision integers match Coq's Z type for concrete computations.
Both compute whether any of the three Pythagorean equalities holds and check res equals that boolean; Python ints match Coq Z and == matches Z.eqb, so results align for all inputs.
Both compute the same boolean condition (any of the three Pythagorean equalities) and check res equals that value, using unbounded integers and standard boolean or/equality.
Both compute whether res equals the disjunction of the three Pythagorean equalities using exact integer arithmetic and booleans; no overflow or semantic differences.
